risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables." Comment: The study explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer-generated random tables), which supports truly random allocation.

Quote: "Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables." Comment: This describes the random sequence generation method only. The report does not state how the allocation was concealed from the person enrolling participants (e.g. central randomization, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, pharmacy-controlled allocation). Therefore, allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A total of eight weekly sessions were held for the MBSR group... Following that, the MBSR program was carried out with patients in the intervention group spread over 8–10 weeks’ duration. Patients in the TAU group did not receive any further sessions after the health education session." Comment: The intervention and control conditions were obviously different, with one group receiving repeated mindfulness sessions and home practice materials while the TAU group received no further sessions, so participants and personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded. Knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect key study outcomes, especially subjective self-reported outcomes such as anxiety, depression, and perceived stress (HADS, PSS).

Quote: "The Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) was used to assess anxiety and depressive symptoms in patients with CHD... Perceived stress was assessed using the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS)." and "BP of the patients was measured either by the cardiologist or a physician within 1 week of pre-assessment, post-assessment and at follow-up using sphygmomanometer." Comment: Outcomes included patient-reported subjective measures (HADS anxiety/depression and PSS perceived stress), so the outcome assessor for these outcomes was the patient self-report. The paper does not report participant blinding, and given the nature of MBSR versus treatment-as-usual, participants were effectively aware of allocation ("the MBSR program was carried out with patients in the intervention group... Patients in the TAU group did not receive any further sessions"). Under the worst-outcome rule, these unblinded self-reported subjective outcomes make detection bias High risk, even though BP/BMI are more objective.

Quote: "Of the 30 patients, there were a total of 17 available at 3-month follow-up (12 in the MBSR and five in the TAU group)." Comment: The trial randomized 15 participants per arm, so by 3-month follow-up 3/15 (20%) were missing from MBSR versus 10/15 (66.7%) missing from TAU. This is very high overall attrition (13/30 = 43.3%) with a very large between-group imbalance (46.7 percentage points), which is sufficient positive evidence for attrition bias on follow-up outcomes (e.g. mental distress, BP, BMI). Although the paper states that baseline scores of follow-up completers and non-completers did not differ, the reported follow-up loss remains substantial and differential, and no robust missing-data method is described.
